revision.text:
[[File:Yahiko shrine.JPG|thumb|right|Yahiko Shrine]] {{nihongo|Yahiko jinja|弥彦神社||}} is the [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Yahiko, Niigata]]. It is located at the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]] in the middle of [[Sado Yahiko Quasi-National Park]].<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
==History== Yahiko shrine was already well established when a new building was created by Imperial edict in the 8th century.<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> The shrine honors Ameno-Kaguyama-no-Mikoto, who is said to have taught people how to produce salt from seawater.<ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
The present shrine was rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
==Related pages== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links == {{Commons category-inline|Yahiko-jinja}} * [http://zappy.kyarame.com/other/08/yahiko/emperor/emperor_124/emperor.htm 歴代天皇御肖像画 (Imperial Portraits)]
{{Shinto shrine}}
<!-- {{coord|_|_|_|N|136|_|_|E|source:___|display=title}} -->
{{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-struct-stub}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines]]
[[ja:彌彦神社]] [[ko:이야히코 신사]] [[zh:彌彥神社]]

[[File:Yahiko shrine.JPG|thumb|right|Yahiko Shrine]] {{nihongo|Yahiko jinja|弥彦神社||}} is the [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Yahiko, Niigata]]. It is located at the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]] in the middle of [[Sado Yahiko Quasi-National Park]].<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
==History== Yahiko shrine was already well established when a new building was created by Imperial edict in the 8th century.<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> The shrine honors Ameno-Kaguyama-no-Mikoto, who is said to have taught people how to produce salt from seawater.<ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
Yahiko was one of the chief [[Shinto shrine]]s (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of the old [[Echigo Province]]. It serves today as one of the ichinomiya of [[Niigata Prefecture]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref>
The present shrine was rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
==Related pages== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links == {{Commons category-inline|Yahiko-jinja}} * [http://zappy.kyarame.com/other/08/yahiko/emperor/emperor_124/emperor.htm 歴代天皇御肖像画 (Imperial Portraits)]
{{Shinto shrine}}
<!-- {{coord|_|_|_|N|136|_|_|E|source:___|display=title}} -->

[[File:Yahiko shrine.JPG|thumb|right|Yahiko Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Yahiko jinja'''|弥彦神社||}}, also known as '''Iyahiko-jinja''' is the [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Yahiko, Niigata]]. Yahiko was one of the chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of the old [[Echigo Province]]. [[File:Yahiko shrine.JPG|thumb|right|Yahiko Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Yahiko jinja'''|弥彦神社||}}, also known as '''Iyahiko-jinja''' is the [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Yahiko, Niigata]]. It is located at the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]] in the middle of [[Sado Yahiko Quasi-National Park]].<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
==History== Yahiko shrine was already well established when a new building was created by Imperial edict in the 8th century.<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is * {{nihongo|Ameno-Kaguyama-no-Mikoto|天香山命||}} who is said to have taught people how to produce salt from seawater.<ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
Yahiko was one of the chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of the old [[Echigo Province]]. It serves today as one of the ''ichinomiya'' of [[Niigata Prefecture]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref>
The present shrine was rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>

revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = {{nihongo|'''Yahiko jinja'''|弥彦神社||}} | image = Yahiko shrine.JPG | caption = Yahiko Shrine | type = | dedication = | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | location_map = Japan | coordinates ={{coord|37|42|23.7|N|138|49|33.9|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | address = | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Yahiko jinja'''|弥彦神社||}}, also known as '''Iyahiko-jinja''' is the [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Yahiko, Niigata]]. It is located at the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]] in the middle of [[Sado Yahiko Quasi-National Park]].<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
The solemn shrine standing at the tranquil foot of a mountain is popularly known as a power spot for love and good fortune<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/niigata/yahiko-shrine/information|title=About Yahiko Shrine - Niigata Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-10-14}}</ref>
==History== Yahiko shrine was already well established when a new building was created by Imperial edict in the 8th century.<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is * {{nihongo|Ameno-Kaguyama-no-Mikoto|天香山命||}} who is said to have taught fishing, rice cultivation, salt production and the other basics of farming to the residents as well as helping to build the nation<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/niigata/yahiko-shrine/information|title=About Yahiko Shrine - Niigata Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-10-14}}</ref>.<ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
Yahiko was one of the chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of the old [[Echigo Province]]. It serves today as one of the ''ichinomiya'' of [[Niigata Prefecture]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref>
The present shrine was rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
==Related pages== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links == {{Commons category-inline|Yahiko-jinja}} * [http://zappy.kyarame.com/other/08/yahiko/emperor/emperor_124/emperor.htm 歴代天皇御肖像画 (Imperial Portraits)]
{{Shinto shrine}}
{{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-struct-stub}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]]

The present shrine was rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Yahiko Shrine |native_name = 弥彦神社 | image = Yahiko shrine.JPG | caption = Haiden of Yahiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|42|23.7|N|138|49|33.9|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yahiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-Mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Yahiko, Niigata]], Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = February 2 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yahiko jinja'''|弥彦神社||}}, also known as '''Iyahiko-jinja''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Yahiko neighborhood of the village of [[Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], [[Nishikanbara District, Niigata|Nishikanbara District]], [[Niigata Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the three shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The shrine's annual festival is held on February 2.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref> This shrine standing at the foot of a mountain is popularly known as a power spot for love and good fortune<ref name="planetyze.com">{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/niigata/yahiko-shrine/information|title=About Yahiko Shrine - Niigata Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-10-14}}</ref>
The shrine is located within [[Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park]] and is on the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]],<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> a 634-meter [[Sacred mountains|sacred mountain]] which forms the ''[[shintai]]'' of the shrine.
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Yahiko Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto'''|天香山命||}}
==History== The foundation date of Yahiko Shrine is unknown, but the shrine dates to prehistoric times as it us referred to as "ancient" in a poem even in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Man'yōshū]]''. Per the shrine's legend, Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto landed from the heavens at Nozumihama (in what is now the city of [[Nagaoka, Niigata|Nagaoka]]) and taught local people about industries such as fishing, salt production, rice cultivation, and [[sericulture]].<ref name="planetyze.com"/><ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> He was later was enshrined on Mount Yahiko as the ''kami'' who founded Echigo. He also was recorded in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as having performed a ''[[bugaku]]'' dance at the coronation of [[Emperor Jimmu]]. Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto is also claimed to be the ancestor of the Owari ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' and it is more than likely that the shrine legend and tradition confuses this ''kami'' with Prince Ohiko (大彦命), the ancestor of the Hokuriku ''Kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is mentioned in and entry for 833 AD in ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and per the same source, the shrine was awarded the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) in 842 AD. Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD. The subsequent history of the shrine is uncertain and often contradictory, as most old records have been lost in fires and other disasters over the years. The shrine was well patronised by the military class, and the shrine treasury has a [[Muromachi period]] [[Ōdachi]] [[Japanese sword]] which is an [[Important Cultural Property of Japan]] as well as amor and swords donated by [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Uesugi Kenshin]].<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
During the [[Edo period]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Takada Domain]], [[Matsudaira Tadateru]] granted the shrine estates with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''koku'' for its upkeep/ During the [[Edo Period]], the ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar [[Hirata Atsutane]] claimed that the shrine had preserved in ''[[Jindai moji]]'', predating the introduction of Chinese-based ''[[kanji]]'', but that these records had been lost in a fire.
After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|Kokuhei Chūsha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Yahiko Station]] o the [[JR East]] [[Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:弥彦山.jpg|Mount Yahiko File:Yahiko Jinja2008-2.jpg|Torii Iyahiko-jinja Tohashira-jinja.JPG|Tobashira Jinja Shaden </gallery>
==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社境内末社十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Ichinomiya]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links == {{Commons category-inline|Iyahiko-jinja}} *[http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp Official home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/7476 Niigata Prefecture Kanko Navi] {{in lang|ja}}

revision.comment: Moving [[:Category:Beppo Shrines]] to [[:Category:Beppyo shrines]] per [[Wikipedia:Categories for discussion/Log/2022 June 2#Category:Beppo Shrines]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yahiko Shrine |native_name = 弥彦神社 | image = Yahiko shrine.JPG | caption = Haiden of Yahiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|42|23.7|N|138|49|33.9|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yahiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-Mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Yahiko, Niigata]], Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = February 2 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yahiko jinja'''|弥彦神社||}}, also known as '''Iyahiko-jinja''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Yahiko neighborhood of the village of [[Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], [[Nishikanbara District, Niigata|Nishikanbara District]], [[Niigata Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the three shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The shrine's annual festival is held on February 2.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref> This shrine standing at the foot of a mountain is popularly known as a power spot for love and good fortune<ref name="planetyze.com">{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/niigata/yahiko-shrine/information|title=About Yahiko Shrine - Niigata Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-10-14}}</ref>
The shrine is located within [[Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park]] and is on the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]],<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> a 634-meter [[Sacred mountains|sacred mountain]] which forms the ''[[shintai]]'' of the shrine.
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Yahiko Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto'''|天香山命||}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]]
==History== The foundation date of Yahiko Shrine is unknown, but the shrine dates to prehistoric times as it us referred to as "ancient" in a poem even in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Man'yōshū]]''. Per the shrine's legend, Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto landed from the heavens at Nozumihama (in what is now the city of [[Nagaoka, Niigata|Nagaoka]]) and taught local people about industries such as fishing, salt production, rice cultivation, and [[sericulture]].<ref name="planetyze.com"/><ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> He was later was enshrined on Mount Yahiko as the ''kami'' who founded Echigo. He also was recorded in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as having performed a ''[[bugaku]]'' dance at the coronation of [[Emperor Jimmu]]. Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto is also claimed to be the ancestor of the Owari ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' and it is more than likely that the shrine legend and tradition confuses this ''kami'' with Prince Ohiko (大彦命), the ancestor of the Hokuriku ''Kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is mentioned in and entry for 833 AD in ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and per the same source, the shrine was awarded the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) in 842 AD. Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD. The subsequent history of the shrine is uncertain and often contradictory, as most old records have been lost in fires and other disasters over the years. The shrine was well patronised by the military class, and the shrine treasury has a [[Muromachi period]] [[Ōdachi]] [[Japanese sword]] which is an [[Important Cultural Property of Japan]] as well as amor and swords donated by [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Uesugi Kenshin]].<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
During the [[Edo period]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Takada Domain]], [[Matsudaira Tadateru]] granted the shrine estates with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''koku'' for its upkeep/ During the [[Edo Period]], the ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar [[Hirata Atsutane]] claimed that the shrine had preserved in ''[[Jindai moji]]'', predating the introduction of Chinese-based ''[[kanji]]'', but that these records had been lost in a fire.
After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|Kokuhei Chūsha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Yahiko Station]] o the [[JR East]] [[Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:弥彦山.jpg|Mount Yahiko File:Yahiko Jinja2008-2.jpg|Torii Iyahiko-jinja Tohashira-jinja.JPG|Tobashira Jinja Shaden </gallery>
==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社境内末社十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Ichinomiya]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links == {{Commons category-inline|Iyahiko-jinja}} *[http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp Official home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/7476 Niigata Prefecture Kanko Navi] {{in lang|ja}}
{{Shinto shrine}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Echigo Province]] [[Category:Yahiko, Niigata]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]]

*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>

==External links == {{Commons category-inline|Iyahiko-jinja}} *[http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp Official home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/7476 Niigata Prefecture Kanko Navi] {{in lang|ja}}
{{Shinto shrine}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Echigo Province]] [[Category:Yahiko, Niigata]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yahiko Shrine |native_name = 弥彦神社 | image = Yahiko shrine.JPG | caption = Haiden of Yahiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|42|23.7|N|138|49|33.9|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yahiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-Mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Yahiko, Niigata]], Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = February 2 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yahiko jinja'''|弥彦神社||}}, also known as '''Iyahiko-jinja''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Yahiko neighborhood of the village of [[Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], [[Nishikanbara District, Niigata|Nishikanbara District]], [[Niigata Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the three shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The shrine's annual festival is held on February 2.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref> This shrine standing at the foot of a mountain is popularly known as a power spot for love and good fortune<ref name="planetyze.com">{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/niigata/yahiko-shrine/information|title=About Yahiko Shrine - Niigata Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-10-14}}</ref>
The shrine is located within [[Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park]] and is on the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]],<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> a 634-meter [[Sacred mountains|sacred mountain]] which forms the ''[[shintai]]'' of the shrine.
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Yahiko Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto'''|天香山命||}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]]
==History== The foundation date of Yahiko Shrine is unknown, but the shrine dates to prehistoric times as it us referred to as "ancient" in a poem even in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Man'yōshū]]''. Per the shrine's legend, Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto landed from the heavens at Nozumihama (in what is now the city of [[Nagaoka, Niigata|Nagaoka]]) and taught local people about industries such as fishing, salt production, rice cultivation, and [[sericulture]].<ref name="planetyze.com"/><ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> He was later was enshrined on Mount Yahiko as the ''kami'' who founded Echigo. He also was recorded in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as having performed a ''[[bugaku]]'' dance at the coronation of [[Emperor Jimmu]]. Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto is also claimed to be the ancestor of the Owari ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' and it is more than likely that the shrine legend and tradition confuses this ''kami'' with Prince Ohiko (大彦命), the ancestor of the Hokuriku ''Kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is mentioned in and entry for 833 AD in ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and per the same source, the shrine was awarded the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) in 842 AD. Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD. The subsequent history of the shrine is uncertain and often contradictory, as most old records have been lost in fires and other disasters over the years. The shrine was well patronised by the military class, and the shrine treasury has a [[Muromachi period]] [[Ōdachi]] [[Japanese sword]] which is an [[Important Cultural Property of Japan]] as well as amor and swords donated by [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Uesugi Kenshin]].<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161113214513/http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html |date=2016-11-13 }}; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
During the [[Edo period]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Takada Domain]], [[Matsudaira Tadateru]] granted the shrine estates with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''koku'' for its upkeep/ During the [[Edo Period]], the ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar [[Hirata Atsutane]] claimed that the shrine had preserved in ''[[Jindai moji]]'', predating the introduction of Chinese-based ''[[kanji]]'', but that these records had been lost in a fire.
After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|Kokuhei Chūsha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Yahiko Station]] o the [[JR East]] [[Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:弥彦山.jpg|Mount Yahiko File:Yahiko Jinja2008-2.jpg|Torii Iyahiko-jinja Tohashira-jinja.JPG|Tobashira Jinja Shaden </gallery>
==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社境内末社十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>

Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD. The subsequent history of the shrine is uncertain and often contradictory, as most old records have been lost in fires and other disasters over the years. The shrine was well patronised by the military class, and the shrine treasury has a [[Muromachi period]] [[Ōdachi]] [[Japanese sword]] which is an [[Important Cultural Property of Japan]] as well as amor and swords donated by [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Uesugi Kenshin]].<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161113214513/http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html |date=2016-11-13 }}; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
During the [[Edo period]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Takada Domain]], [[Matsudaira Tadateru]] granted the shrine estates with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''koku'' for its upkeep/ During the [[Edo Period]], the ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar [[Hirata Atsutane]] claimed that the shrine had preserved in ''[[Jindai moji]]'', predating the introduction of Chinese-based ''[[kanji]]'', but that these records had been lost in a fire.
After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|Kokuhei Chūsha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Yahiko Station]] o the [[JR East]] [[Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:弥彦山.jpg|Mount Yahiko File:Yahiko Jinja2008-2.jpg|Torii Iyahiko-jinja Tohashira-jinja.JPG|Tobashira Jinja Shaden </gallery>
==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社境内末社十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yahiko Shrine |native_name = 弥彦神社 | image = Yahiko shrine.JPG | caption = Haiden of Yahiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|42|23.7|N|138|49|33.9|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yahiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-Mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Yahiko, Niigata]], Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = February 2 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yahiko jinja'''|弥彦神社||}}, also known as '''Iyahiko-jinja''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Yahiko neighborhood of the village of [[Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], [[Nishikanbara District, Niigata|Nishikanbara District]], [[Niigata Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the three shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The shrine's annual festival is held on February 2.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref> This shrine standing at the foot of a mountain is popularly known as a power spot for love and good fortune<ref name="planetyze.com">{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/niigata/yahiko-shrine/information|title=About Yahiko Shrine - Niigata Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-10-14}}</ref>
The shrine is located within [[Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park]] and is on the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]],<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> a 634-meter [[Sacred mountains|sacred mountain]] which forms the ''[[shintai]]'' of the shrine.
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Yahiko Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto'''|天香山命||}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]]
==History== The foundation date of Yahiko Shrine is unknown, but the shrine dates to prehistoric times as it us referred to as "ancient" in a poem even in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Man'yōshū]]''. Per the shrine's legend, Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto landed from the heavens at Nozumihama (in what is now the city of [[Nagaoka, Niigata|Nagaoka]]) and taught local people about industries such as fishing, salt production, rice cultivation, and [[sericulture]].<ref name="planetyze.com"/><ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> He was later was enshrined on Mount Yahiko as the ''kami'' who founded Echigo. He also was recorded in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as having performed a ''[[bugaku]]'' dance at the coronation of [[Emperor Jimmu]]. Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto is also claimed to be the ancestor of the Owari ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' and it is more than likely that the shrine legend and tradition confuses this ''kami'' with Prince Ohiko (大彦命), the ancestor of the Hokuriku ''Kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is mentioned in and entry for 833 AD in ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and per the same source, the shrine was awarded the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) in 842 AD. Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD. The subsequent history of the shrine is uncertain and often contradictory, as most old records have been lost in fires and other disasters over the years. The shrine was well patronised by the military class, and the shrine treasury has a [[Muromachi period]] [[Ōdachi]] [[Japanese sword]] which is an [[Important Cultural Property of Japan]] as well as amor and swords donated by [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Uesugi Kenshin]].<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161113214513/http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html |date=2016-11-13 }}; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
During the [[Edo period]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Takada Domain]], [[Matsudaira Tadateru]] granted the shrine estates with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''koku'' for its upkeep/ During the [[Edo Period]], the ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar [[Hirata Atsutane]] claimed that the shrine had preserved in ''[[Jindai moji]]'', predating the introduction of Chinese-based ''[[kanji]]'', but that these records had been lost in a fire.
After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Yahiko Station]] o the [[JR East]] [[Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:弥彦山.jpg|Mount Yahiko File:Yahiko Jinja2008-2.jpg|Torii Iyahiko-jinja Tohashira-jinja.JPG|Tobashira Jinja Shaden </gallery>
==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社境内末社十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>

The shrine is located within [[Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park]] and is on the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]],<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> a 634-meter [[Sacred mountains|sacred mountain]] which forms the ''[[shintai]]'' of the shrine.
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Yahiko Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto'''|天香山命||}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]]
==History== The foundation date of Yahiko Shrine is unknown, but the shrine dates to prehistoric times as it us referred to as "ancient" in a poem even in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Man'yōshū]]''. Per the shrine's legend, Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto landed from the heavens at Nozumihama (in what is now the city of [[Nagaoka, Niigata|Nagaoka]]) and taught local people about industries such as fishing, salt production, rice cultivation, and [[sericulture]].<ref name="planetyze.com"/><ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> He was later enshrined on Mount Yahiko as the ''kami'' who founded Echigo. He also was recorded in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as having performed a ''[[bugaku]]'' dance at the coronation of [[Emperor Jimmu]]. Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto is also claimed to be the ancestor of the Owari ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' and it is more than likely that the shrine legend and tradition confuses this ''kami'' with Prince Ohiko (大彦命), the ancestor of the Hokuriku ''Kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is mentioned in and entry for 833 AD in ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and per the same source, the shrine was awarded the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) in 842 AD. Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD. The subsequent history of the shrine is uncertain and often contradictory, as most old records have been lost in fires and other disasters over the years. The shrine was well patronised by the military class, and the shrine treasury has a [[Muromachi period]] [[Ōdachi]] [[Japanese sword]] which is an [[Important Cultural Property of Japan]] as well as amor and swords donated by [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Uesugi Kenshin]].<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161113214513/http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html |date=2016-11-13 }}; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
During the [[Edo period]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Takada Domain]], [[Matsudaira Tadateru]] granted the shrine estates with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''koku'' for its upkeep/ During the [[Edo Period]], the ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar [[Hirata Atsutane]] claimed that the shrine had preserved in ''[[Jindai moji]]'', predating the introduction of Chinese-based ''[[kanji]]'', but that these records had been lost in a fire.
After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Yahiko Station]] o the [[JR East]] [[Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:弥彦山.jpg|Mount Yahiko File:Yahiko Jinja2008-2.jpg|Torii Iyahiko-jinja Tohashira-jinja.JPG|Tobashira Jinja Shaden </gallery>
==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社境内末社十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Ichinomiya]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links == {{Commons category-inline|Iyahiko-jinja}} *[http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp Official home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/7476 Niigata Prefecture Kanko Navi] {{in lang|ja}}

revision.comment: 36 revisions imported from [[:wikipedia:Yahiko_Shrine]]: Importing from English Wikipedia for the purposes of this wiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yahiko Shrine |native_name = 弥彦神社 | image = Yahiko shrine.JPG | caption = Haiden of Yahiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|42|23.7|N|138|49|33.9|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yahiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-Mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Yahiko, Niigata]], Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = February 2 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yahiko jinja'''|弥彦神社||}}, also known as '''Iyahiko-jinja''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Yahiko neighborhood of the village of [[Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], [[Nishikanbara District, Niigata|Nishikanbara District]], [[Niigata Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the three shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The shrine's annual festival is held on February 2.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref> This shrine standing at the foot of a mountain is popularly known as a power spot for love and good fortune<ref name="planetyze.com">{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/niigata/yahiko-shrine/information|title=About Yahiko Shrine - Niigata Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-10-14}}</ref>
The shrine is located within [[Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park]] and is on the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]],<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> a 634-meter [[Sacred mountains|sacred mountain]] which forms the ''[[shintai]]'' of the shrine.
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Yahiko Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto'''|天香山命||}}
==History== The foundation date of Yahiko Shrine is unknown, but the shrine dates to prehistoric times as it us referred to as "ancient" in a poem even in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Man'yōshū]]''. Per the shrine's legend, Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto landed from the heavens at Nozumihama (in what is now the city of [[Nagaoka, Niigata|Nagaoka]]) and taught local people about industries such as fishing, salt production, rice cultivation, and [[sericulture]].<ref name="planetyze.com"/><ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> He was later enshrined on Mount Yahiko as the ''kami'' who founded Echigo. He also was recorded in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as having performed a ''[[bugaku]]'' dance at the coronation of [[Emperor Jimmu]]. Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto is also claimed to be the ancestor of the Owari ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' and it is more than likely that the shrine legend and tradition confuses this ''kami'' with Prince Ohiko (大彦命), the ancestor of the Hokuriku ''Kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is mentioned in and entry for 833 AD in ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and per the same source, the shrine was awarded the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) in 842 AD. Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD. The subsequent history of the shrine is uncertain and often contradictory, as most old records have been lost in fires and other disasters over the years. The shrine was well patronised by the military class, and the shrine treasury has a [[Muromachi period]] [[Ōdachi]] [[Japanese sword]] which is an [[Important Cultural Property of Japan]] as well as amor and swords donated by [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Uesugi Kenshin]].<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161113214513/http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html |date=2016-11-13 }}; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
During the [[Edo period]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Takada Domain]], [[Matsudaira Tadateru]] granted the shrine estates with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''koku'' for its upkeep/ During the [[Edo Period]], the ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar [[Hirata Atsutane]] claimed that the shrine had preserved in ''[[Jindai moji]]'', predating the introduction of Chinese-based ''[[kanji]]'', but that these records had been lost in a fire.
After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Yahiko Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:弥彦山.jpg|Mount Yahiko File:Yahiko Jinja2008-2.jpg|Torii Iyahiko-jinja Tohashira-jinja.JPG|Tobashira Jinja Shaden </gallery>
==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社境内末社十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Ichinomiya]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links == {{Commons category-inline|Iyahiko-jinja}} *[http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp Official home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/7476 Niigata Prefecture Kanko Navi] {{in lang|ja}}
{{Shinto shrine}}

This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社境内末社十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yahiko Shrine |native_name = 弥彦神社 | image = Yahiko shrine.JPG | caption = Haiden of Yahiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|42|23.7|N|138|49|33.9|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yahiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-Mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Yahiko, Niigata]], Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = February 2 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yahiko jinja'''|弥彦神社||}}, also known as '''Iyahiko-jinja''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Yahiko neighborhood of the village of [[Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], [[Nishikanbara District, Niigata|Nishikanbara District]], [[Niigata Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the three shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The shrine's annual festival is held on February 2.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref> This shrine standing at the foot of a mountain is popularly known as a power spot for love and good fortune<ref name="planetyze.com">{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/niigata/yahiko-shrine/information|title=About Yahiko Shrine - Niigata Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-10-14}}</ref>
The shrine is located within [[Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park]] and is on the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]],<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> a 634-meter [[Sacred mountains|sacred mountain]] which forms the ''[[shintai]]'' of the shrine.
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Yahiko Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto'''|天香山命||}}
==History== The foundation date of Yahiko Shrine is unknown, but the shrine dates to prehistoric times as it us referred to as "ancient" in a poem even in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Man'yōshū]]''. Per the shrine's legend, Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto landed from the heavens at Nozumihama (in what is now the city of [[Nagaoka, Niigata|Nagaoka]]) and taught local people about industries such as fishing, salt production, rice cultivation, and [[sericulture]].<ref name="planetyze.com"/><ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> He was later enshrined on Mount Yahiko as the ''kami'' who founded Echigo. He also was recorded in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as having performed a ''[[bugaku]]'' dance at the coronation of [[Emperor Jimmu]]. Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto is also claimed to be the ancestor of the Owari ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' and it is more than likely that the shrine legend and tradition confuses this ''kami'' with Prince Ohiko (大彦命), the ancestor of the Hokuriku ''Kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is mentioned in and entry for 833 AD in ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and per the same source, the shrine was awarded the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) in 842 AD. Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD. The subsequent history of the shrine is uncertain and often contradictory, as most old records have been lost in fires and other disasters over the years. The shrine was well patronised by the military class, and the shrine treasury has a [[Muromachi period]] [[Ōdachi]] [[Japanese sword]] which is an [[Important Cultural Property of Japan]] as well as amor and swords donated by [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Uesugi Kenshin]].<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161113214513/http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html |date=2016-11-13 }}; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
During the [[Edo period]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Takada Domain]], [[Matsudaira Tadateru]] granted the shrine estates with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''koku'' for its upkeep/ During the [[Edo Period]], the ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar [[Hirata Atsutane]] claimed that the shrine had preserved in ''[[Jindai moji]]'', predating the introduction of Chinese-based ''[[kanji]]'', but that these records had been lost in a fire.
After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Yahiko Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:弥彦山.jpg|Mount Yahiko File:Yahiko Jinja2008-2.jpg|Torii Iyahiko-jinja Tohashira-jinja.JPG|Tobashira Jinja Shaden </gallery>
==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社境内末社十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Ichinomiya]]

Per the shrine's legend, Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto landed from the heavens at Nozumihama (in what is now the city of [[Nagaoka, Niigata|Nagaoka]]) and taught local people about industries such as fishing, salt production, rice cultivation, and [[sericulture]].<ref name="planetyze.com"/><ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> He was later enshrined on Mount Yahiko as the ''kami'' who founded Echigo. He also was recorded in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as having performed a ''[[bugaku]]'' dance at the coronation of [[Emperor Jimmu]]. Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto is also claimed to be the ancestor of the Owari ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' and it is more than likely that the shrine legend and tradition confuses this ''kami'' with Prince Ohiko (大彦命), the ancestor of the Hokuriku ''Kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is mentioned in and entry for 833 AD in ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and per the same source, the shrine was awarded the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) in 842 AD. Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD. The subsequent history of the shrine is uncertain and often contradictory, as most old records have been lost in fires and other disasters over the years. The shrine was well patronised by the military class, and the shrine treasury has a [[Muromachi period]] [[Ōdachi]] [[Japanese sword]] which is an [[Important Cultural Property of Japan]] as well as amor and swords donated by [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Uesugi Kenshin]].<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161113214513/http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html |date=2016-11-13 }}; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
During the [[Edo period]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Takada Domain]], [[Matsudaira Tadateru]] granted the shrine estates with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''koku'' for its upkeep/ During the [[Edo Period]], the ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar [[Hirata Atsutane]] claimed that the shrine had preserved in ''[[Jindai moji]]'', predating the introduction of Chinese-based ''[[kanji]]'', but that these records had been lost in a fire.
After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Yahiko Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:弥彦山.jpg|Mount Yahiko File:Yahiko Jinja2008-2.jpg|Torii Iyahiko-jinja Tohashira-jinja.JPG|Tobashira Jinja Shaden </gallery>
==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社境内末社十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yahiko Shrine |native_name = 弥彦神社 | image = Yahiko shrine.JPG | caption = Haiden of Yahiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|42|23.7|N|138|49|33.9|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yahiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-Mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Yahiko, Niigata]], Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = February 2 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yahiko jinja'''|弥彦神社||}}, also known as '''Iyahiko-jinja''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Yahiko neighborhood of the village of [[Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], [[Nishikanbara District, Niigata|Nishikanbara District]], [[Niigata Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the three shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The shrine's annual festival is held on February 2.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref> This shrine standing at the foot of a mountain is popularly known as a power spot for love and good fortune<ref name="planetyze.com">{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/niigata/yahiko-shrine/information|title=About Yahiko Shrine - Niigata Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-10-14}}</ref>
The shrine is located within [[Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park]] and is on the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]],<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> a 634-meter [[Sacred mountains|sacred mountain]] which forms the ''[[shintai]]'' of the shrine.
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Yahiko Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto'''|天香山命||}}
==History== The foundation date of Yahiko Shrine is unknown, but the shrine dates to prehistoric times as it us referred to as "ancient" in a poem even in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Man'yōshū]]''. Per the shrine's legend, Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto landed from the heavens at Nozumihama (in what is now the city of [[Nagaoka, Niigata|Nagaoka]]) and taught local people about industries such as fishing, salt production, rice cultivation, and [[sericulture]].<ref name="planetyze.com"/><ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> He was later enshrined on Mount Yahiko as the ''kami'' who founded Echigo. He also was recorded in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as having performed a ''[[bugaku]]'' dance at the coronation of [[Emperor Jimmu]]. Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto is also claimed to be the ancestor of the Owari ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' and it is more than likely that the shrine legend and tradition confuses this ''kami'' with Prince Ohiko (大彦命), the ancestor of the Hokuriku ''Kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is mentioned in and entry for 833 AD in ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and per the same source, the shrine was awarded the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) in 842 AD. Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD. The subsequent history of the shrine is uncertain and often contradictory, as most old records have been lost in fires and other disasters over the years. The shrine was well patronised by the military class, and the shrine treasury has a [[Muromachi period]] [[Ōdachi]] [[Japanese sword]] which is an [[Important Cultural Property of Japan]] as well as amor and swords donated by [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Uesugi Kenshin]].<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161113214513/http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html |date=2016-11-13 }}; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
During the [[Edo period]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Takada Domain]], [[Matsudaira Tadateru]] granted the shrine estates with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''koku'' for its upkeep/ During the [[Edo Period]], the ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar [[Hirata Atsutane]] claimed that the shrine had preserved in ''[[Jindai moji]]'', predating the introduction of Chinese-based ''[[kanji]]'', but that these records had been lost in a fire.
After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Yahiko Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社境内末社十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
