{{Short description|Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yahiko Shrine |native_name = 弥彦神社 | image = Yahiko shrine.JPG | caption = Haiden of Yahiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|42|23.7|N|138|49|33.9|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yahiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-Mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko, Niigata]], Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = February 2 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yahiko jinja'''|弥彦神社||}}, also known as '''Iyahiko-jinja''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Yahiko neighborhood of the village of [[:en:Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], [[:en:Nishikanbara District, Niigata|Nishikanbara District]], [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the three shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The shrine's annual festival is held on February 2.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref> This shrine standing at the foot of a mountain is popularly known as a power spot for love and good fortune<ref name="planetyze.com">{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/niigata/yahiko-shrine/information|title=About Yahiko Shrine - Niigata Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-10-14}}</ref> The shrine is located within [[:en:Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park|Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park]] and is on the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]],<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> a 634-meter [[:en:Sacred mountains|sacred mountain]] which forms the ''[[shintai]]'' of the shrine.
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Yahiko Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto'''|天香山命||}}
==History== The foundation date of Yahiko Shrine is unknown, but the shrine dates to prehistoric times as it us referred to as "ancient" in a poem even in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]''. Per the shrine's legend, Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto landed from the heavens at Nozumihama (in what is now the city of [[:en:Nagaoka, Niigata|Nagaoka]]) and taught local people about industries such as fishing, salt production, rice cultivation, and [[:en:Sericulture|Sericulture]].<ref name="planetyze.com"/><ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> He was later enshrined on Mount Yahiko as the ''kami'' who founded Echigo. He also was recorded in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as having performed a ''[[:en:Bugaku|Bugaku]]'' dance at the coronation of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]. Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto is also claimed to be the ancestor of the Owari ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' and it is more than likely that the shrine legend and tradition confuses this ''kami'' with Prince Ohiko (大彦命), the ancestor of the Hokuriku ''Kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is mentioned in and entry for 833 AD in ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and per the same source, the shrine was awarded the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) in 842 AD. Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD. The subsequent history of the shrine is uncertain and often contradictory, as most old records have been lost in fires and other disasters over the years. The shrine was well patronised by the military class, and the shrine treasury has a [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] [[:en:Ōdachi|Ōdachi]] [[Japanese sword]] which is an [[:en:Important Cultural Property of Japan|Important Cultural Property of Japan]] as well as amor and swords donated by [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]].<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161113214513/http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html |date=2016-11-13 }}; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Takada Domain|Takada Domain]], [[Matsudaira Tadateru]] granted the shrine estates with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''koku'' for its upkeep/ During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholar [[Hirata Atsutane]] claimed that the shrine had preserved in ''[[:en:Jindai moji|Jindai moji]]'', predating the introduction of Chinese-based ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'', but that these records had been lost in a fire.
After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Yahiko Station|Yahiko Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Yahiko Line|Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社Keidai-Massha十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>

*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q195684]] Wikidata name: Yahiko Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yahiko Shrine |native_name = 弥彦神社 | image = Yahiko shrine.JPG | caption = Haiden of Yahiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|42|23.7|N|138|49|33.9|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yahiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-Mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko, Niigata]], Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = February 2 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yahiko jinja'''|弥彦神社||}}, also known as '''Iyahiko-jinja''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Yahiko neighborhood of the village of [[:en:Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], [[:en:Nishikanbara District, Niigata|Nishikanbara District]], [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the three shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The shrine's annual festival is held on February 2.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref> This shrine standing at the foot of a mountain is popularly known as a power spot for love and good fortune<ref name="planetyze.com">{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/niigata/yahiko-shrine/information|title=About Yahiko Shrine - Niigata Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-10-14}}</ref> The shrine is located within [[:en:Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park|Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park]] and is on the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]],<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> a 634-meter [[:en:Sacred mountains|sacred mountain]] which forms the ''[[shintai]]'' of the shrine.
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Yahiko Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto'''|天香山命||}}
==History== The foundation date of Yahiko Shrine is unknown, but the shrine dates to prehistoric times as it us referred to as "ancient" in a poem even in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]''. Per the shrine's legend, Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto landed from the heavens at Nozumihama (in what is now the city of [[:en:Nagaoka, Niigata|Nagaoka]]) and taught local people about industries such as fishing, salt production, rice cultivation, and [[:en:Sericulture|Sericulture]].<ref name="planetyze.com"/><ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> He was later enshrined on Mount Yahiko as the ''kami'' who founded Echigo. He also was recorded in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as having performed a ''[[:en:Bugaku|Bugaku]]'' dance at the coronation of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]. Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto is also claimed to be the ancestor of the Owari ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' and it is more than likely that the shrine legend and tradition confuses this ''kami'' with Prince Ohiko (大彦命), the ancestor of the Hokuriku ''Kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is mentioned in and entry for 833 AD in ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and per the same source, the shrine was awarded the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) in 842 AD. Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD. The subsequent history of the shrine is uncertain and often contradictory, as most old records have been lost in fires and other disasters over the years. The shrine was well patronised by the military class, and the shrine treasury has a [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] [[:en:Ōdachi|Ōdachi]] [[Japanese sword]] which is an [[:en:Important Cultural Property of Japan|Important Cultural Property of Japan]] as well as amor and swords donated by [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]].<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161113214513/http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html |date=2016-11-13 }}; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Takada Domain|Takada Domain]], [[Matsudaira Tadateru]] granted the shrine estates with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''koku'' for its upkeep/ During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholar [[Hirata Atsutane]] claimed that the shrine had preserved in ''[[:en:Jindai moji|Jindai moji]]'', predating the introduction of Chinese-based ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'', but that these records had been lost in a fire.
After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Yahiko Station|Yahiko Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Yahiko Line|Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:弥彦山.jpg|Mount Yahiko File:Yahiko Jinja2008-2.jpg|Torii Iyahiko-jinja Tohashira-jinja.JPG|Tobashira Jinja Shaden </gallery>
==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社Keidai-Massha十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Ichinomiya]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links == {{Commons category-inline|Iyahiko-jinja}} *[http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp Official home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/7476 Niigata Prefecture Kanko Navi] {{in lang|ja}}
{{Shinto shrine}} ]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q195684]] Wikidata name: Yahiko Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yahiko Shrine |native_name = 弥彦神社 | image = Yahiko shrine.JPG | caption = Haiden of Yahiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|37|42|23.7|N|138|49|33.9|E|region:JP-15_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yahiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-Mikoto | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko, Niigata]], Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = February 2 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yahiko jinja'''|弥彦神社||}}, also known as '''Iyahiko-jinja''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Yahiko neighborhood of the village of [[:en:Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], [[:en:Nishikanbara District, Niigata|Nishikanbara District]], [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the three shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The shrine's annual festival is held on February 2.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref> This shrine standing at the foot of a mountain is popularly known as a power spot for love and good fortune<ref name="planetyze.com">{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/niigata/yahiko-shrine/information|title=About Yahiko Shrine - Niigata Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-10-14}}</ref> The shrine is located within [[:en:Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park|Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park]] and is on the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]],<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> a 634-meter [[:en:Sacred mountains|sacred mountain]] which forms the ''[[shintai]]'' of the shrine.
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Yahiko Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto'''|天香山命||}}
==History== The foundation date of Yahiko Shrine is unknown, but the shrine dates to prehistoric times as it us referred to as "ancient" in a poem even in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]''. Per the shrine's legend, Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto landed from the heavens at Nozumihama (in what is now the city of [[:en:Nagaoka, Niigata|Nagaoka]]) and taught local people about industries such as fishing, salt production, rice cultivation, and [[:en:Sericulture|Sericulture]].<ref name="planetyze.com"/><ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> He was later enshrined on Mount Yahiko as the ''kami'' who founded Echigo. He also was recorded in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as having performed a ''[[:en:Bugaku|Bugaku]]'' dance at the coronation of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]. Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto is also claimed to be the ancestor of the Owari ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' and it is more than likely that the shrine legend and tradition confuses this ''kami'' with Prince Ohiko (大彦命), the ancestor of the Hokuriku ''Kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is mentioned in and entry for 833 AD in ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and per the same source, the shrine was awarded the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) in 842 AD. Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD. The subsequent history of the shrine is uncertain and often contradictory, as most old records have been lost in fires and other disasters over the years. The shrine was well patronised by the military class, and the shrine treasury has a [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] [[:en:Ōdachi|Ōdachi]] [[Japanese sword]] which is an [[:en:Important Cultural Property of Japan|Important Cultural Property of Japan]] as well as amor and swords donated by [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]].<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161113214513/http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html |date=2016-11-13 }}; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Takada Domain|Takada Domain]], [[Matsudaira Tadateru]] granted the shrine estates with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''koku'' for its upkeep/ During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholar [[Hirata Atsutane]] claimed that the shrine had preserved in ''[[:en:Jindai moji|Jindai moji]]'', predating the introduction of Chinese-based ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'', but that these records had been lost in a fire.
After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Yahiko Station|Yahiko Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Yahiko Line|Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:弥彦山.jpg|Mount Yahiko File:Yahiko Jinja2008-2.jpg|Torii Iyahiko-jinja Tohashira-jinja.JPG|Tobashira Jinja Shaden </gallery>
==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社Keidai-Massha十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Ichinomiya]]

Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> a 634-meter [[:en:Sacred mountains|sacred mountain]] which forms the ''[[shintai]]'' of the shrine.
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Yahiko Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto'''|天香山命||}}
==History== The foundation date of Yahiko Shrine is unknown, but the shrine dates to prehistoric times as it us referred to as "ancient" in a poem even in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]''. Per the shrine's legend, Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto landed from the heavens at Nozumihama (in what is now the city of [[:en:Nagaoka, Niigata|Nagaoka]]) and taught local people about industries such as fishing, salt production, rice cultivation, and [[:en:Sericulture|Sericulture]].<ref name="planetyze.com"/><ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> He was later enshrined on Mount Yahiko as the ''kami'' who founded Echigo. He also was recorded in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as having performed a ''[[:en:Bugaku|Bugaku]]'' dance at the coronation of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]. Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto is also claimed to be the ancestor of the Owari ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' and it is more than likely that the shrine legend and tradition confuses this ''kami'' with Prince Ohiko (大彦命), the ancestor of the Hokuriku ''Kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is mentioned in and entry for 833 AD in ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and per the same source, the shrine was awarded the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) in 842 AD. Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD. The subsequent history of the shrine is uncertain and often contradictory, as most old records have been lost in fires and other disasters over the years. The shrine was well patronised by the military class, and the shrine treasury has a [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] [[:en:Ōdachi|Ōdachi]] [[Japanese sword]] which is an [[:en:Important Cultural Property of Japan|Important Cultural Property of Japan]] as well as amor and swords donated by [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]].<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161113214513/http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html |date=2016-11-13 }}; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Takada Domain|Takada Domain]], [[Matsudaira Tadateru]] granted the shrine estates with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''koku'' for its upkeep/ During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholar [[Hirata Atsutane]] claimed that the shrine had preserved in ''[[:en:Jindai moji|Jindai moji]]'', predating the introduction of Chinese-based ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'', but that these records had been lost in a fire.
After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Yahiko Station|Yahiko Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Yahiko Line|Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:弥彦山.jpg|Mount Yahiko File:Yahiko Jinja2008-2.jpg|Torii Iyahiko-jinja Tohashira-jinja.JPG|Tobashira Jinja Shaden </gallery>
==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社Keidai-Massha十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Ichinomiya]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q195684]] Wikidata name: Yahiko Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yahiko Shrine |native_name = 弥彦神社 | image = File:Iyahiko-jinja 1.JPG | caption = Haiden of Yahiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37.7066|N|138.826|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yahiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = {{ill|Amenokaguyamanomikoto|qid=Q10940849}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko, Niigata]], Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = February 2 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} | location = {{ill|Nishikanbara district|qid=Q1155139}}; {{ill|Yahiko|qid=Q1349667}} | part_of = {{ill|San Hikoyama|qid=Q11355405}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yahiko jinja'''|弥彦神社||}}, also known as '''Iyahiko-jinja''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Yahiko neighborhood of the village of [[:en:Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], [[:en:Nishikanbara District, Niigata|Nishikanbara District]], [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the three shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The shrine's annual festival is held on February 2.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref> This shrine standing at the foot of a mountain is popularly known as a power spot for love and good fortune<ref name="planetyze.com">{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/niigata/yahiko-shrine/information|title=About Yahiko Shrine - Niigata Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-10-14}}</ref> The shrine is located within [[:en:Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park|Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park]] and is on the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]],<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> a 634-meter [[:en:Sacred mountains|sacred mountain]] which forms the ''[[shintai]]'' of the shrine.
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Yahiko Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto'''|天香山命||}}
==History== The foundation date of Yahiko Shrine is unknown, but the shrine dates to prehistoric times as it us referred to as "ancient" in a poem even in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]''. Per the shrine's legend, Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto landed from the heavens at Nozumihama (in what is now the city of [[:en:Nagaoka, Niigata|Nagaoka]]) and taught local people about industries such as fishing, salt production, rice cultivation, and [[:en:Sericulture|Sericulture]].<ref name="planetyze.com"/><ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> He was later enshrined on Mount Yahiko as the ''kami'' who founded Echigo. He also was recorded in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as having performed a ''[[:en:Bugaku|Bugaku]]'' dance at the coronation of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]. Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto is also claimed to be the ancestor of the Owari ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' and it is more than likely that the shrine legend and tradition confuses this ''kami'' with Prince Ohiko (大彦命), the ancestor of the Hokuriku ''Kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is mentioned in and entry for 833 AD in ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and per the same source, the shrine was awarded the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) in 842 AD. Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD. The subsequent history of the shrine is uncertain and often contradictory, as most old records have been lost in fires and other disasters over the years. The shrine was well patronised by the military class, and the shrine treasury has a [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] [[:en:Ōdachi|Ōdachi]] [[Japanese sword]] which is an [[:en:Important Cultural Property of Japan|Important Cultural Property of Japan]] as well as amor and swords donated by [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]].<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161113214513/http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html |date=2016-11-13 }}; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Takada Domain|Takada Domain]], [[Matsudaira Tadateru]] granted the shrine estates with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''koku'' for its upkeep/ During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholar [[Hirata Atsutane]] claimed that the shrine had preserved in ''[[:en:Jindai moji|Jindai moji]]'', predating the introduction of Chinese-based ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'', but that these records had been lost in a fire.
After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Yahiko Station|Yahiko Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Yahiko Line|Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:弥彦山.jpg|Mount Yahiko File:Yahiko Jinja2008-2.jpg|Torii Iyahiko-jinja Tohashira-jinja.JPG|Tobashira Jinja Shaden </gallery>
==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社Keidai-Massha十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>

After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Yahiko Station|Yahiko Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Yahiko Line|Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:弥彦山.jpg|Mount Yahiko File:Yahiko Jinja2008-2.jpg|Torii Iyahiko-jinja Tohashira-jinja.JPG|Tobashira Jinja Shaden </gallery>
==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社Keidai-Massha十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Iyahiko-jinja}} [[fr:Yahiko-jinja]] [[ja:彌彦神社]] [[ko:이야히코 신사]] [[zh:彌彥神社]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q195684]] Wikidata name: Yahiko Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yahiko Shrine |native_name = 弥彦神社 | image = File:Iyahiko-jinja 1.JPG | caption = Haiden of Yahiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37.7066|N|138.826|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yahiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Kaguyama|ja|天香山命|zh|天香山命}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko, Niigata]], Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = February 2 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} | location = {{ill|Nishikanbara district|qid=Q1155139}}; {{ill|Yahiko|qid=Q1349667}} | part_of = {{ill|San Hikoyama|qid=Q11355405}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yahiko jinja'''|弥彦神社||}}, also known as '''Iyahiko-jinja''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Yahiko neighborhood of the village of [[:en:Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], [[:en:Nishikanbara District, Niigata|Nishikanbara District]], [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the three shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The shrine's annual festival is held on February 2.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref> This shrine standing at the foot of a mountain is popularly known as a power spot for love and good fortune<ref name="planetyze.com">{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/niigata/yahiko-shrine/information|title=About Yahiko Shrine - Niigata Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-10-14}}</ref> The shrine is located within [[:en:Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park|Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park]] and is on the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]],<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> a 634-meter [[:en:Sacred mountains|sacred mountain]] which forms the ''[[shintai]]'' of the shrine.
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Yahiko Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto'''|天香山命||}}
==History== The foundation date of Yahiko Shrine is unknown, but the shrine dates to prehistoric times as it us referred to as "ancient" in a poem even in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]''. Per the shrine's legend, Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto landed from the heavens at Nozumihama (in what is now the city of [[:en:Nagaoka, Niigata|Nagaoka]]) and taught local people about industries such as fishing, salt production, rice cultivation, and [[:en:Sericulture|Sericulture]].<ref name="planetyze.com"/><ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> He was later enshrined on Mount Yahiko as the ''kami'' who founded Echigo. He also was recorded in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as having performed a ''[[:en:Bugaku|Bugaku]]'' dance at the coronation of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]. Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto is also claimed to be the ancestor of the Owari ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' and it is more than likely that the shrine legend and tradition confuses this ''kami'' with Prince Ohiko (大彦命), the ancestor of the Hokuriku ''Kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is mentioned in and entry for 833 AD in ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and per the same source, the shrine was awarded the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) in 842 AD. Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD. The subsequent history of the shrine is uncertain and often contradictory, as most old records have been lost in fires and other disasters over the years. The shrine was well patronised by the military class, and the shrine treasury has a [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] [[:en:Ōdachi|Ōdachi]] [[Japanese sword]] which is an [[:en:Important Cultural Property of Japan|Important Cultural Property of Japan]] as well as amor and swords donated by [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]].<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161113214513/http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html |date=2016-11-13 }}; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Takada Domain|Takada Domain]], [[Matsudaira Tadateru]] granted the shrine estates with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''koku'' for its upkeep/ During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholar [[Hirata Atsutane]] claimed that the shrine had preserved in ''[[:en:Jindai moji|Jindai moji]]'', predating the introduction of Chinese-based ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'', but that these records had been lost in a fire.
After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Yahiko Station|Yahiko Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Yahiko Line|Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社Keidai-Massha十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Iyahiko-jinja]][[en:Yahiko Shrine]][[fr:Yahiko-jinja]][[ja:彌彦神社]][[ko:이야히코 신사]][[zh:彌彥神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q195684]] Wikidata name: Yahiko Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yahiko Shrine |native_name = 弥彦神社 | image = File:Iyahiko-jinja 1.JPG | caption = Haiden of Yahiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37.7066|N|138.826|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yahiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Kaguyama|ja|天香山命|zh|天香山命}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko, Niigata]], Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = February 2 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} | location = {{ill|Nishikanbara district|qid=Q1155139}}; {{ill|Yahiko|qid=Q1349667}} | part_of = {{ill|San Hikoyama|qid=Q11355405}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yahiko jinja'''|弥彦神社||}}, also known as '''Iyahiko-jinja''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Yahiko neighborhood of the village of [[:en:Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], [[:en:Nishikanbara District, Niigata|Nishikanbara District]], [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the three shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The shrine's annual festival is held on February 2.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref> This shrine standing at the foot of a mountain is popularly known as a power spot for love and good fortune<ref name="planetyze.com">{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/niigata/yahiko-shrine/information|title=About Yahiko Shrine - Niigata Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-10-14}}</ref> The shrine is located within [[:en:Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park|Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park]] and is on the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]],<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> a 634-meter [[:en:Sacred mountains|sacred mountain]] which forms the ''[[shintai]]'' of the shrine.
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Yahiko Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto'''|天香山命||}}
==History== The foundation date of Yahiko Shrine is unknown, but the shrine dates to prehistoric times as it us referred to as "ancient" in a poem even in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]''. Per the shrine's legend, Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto landed from the heavens at Nozumihama (in what is now the city of [[:en:Nagaoka, Niigata|Nagaoka]]) and taught local people about industries such as fishing, salt production, rice cultivation, and [[:en:Sericulture|Sericulture]].<ref name="planetyze.com"/><ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> He was later enshrined on Mount Yahiko as the ''kami'' who founded Echigo. He also was recorded in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as having performed a ''[[:en:Bugaku|Bugaku]]'' dance at the coronation of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]. Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto is also claimed to be the ancestor of the Owari ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' and it is more than likely that the shrine legend and tradition confuses this ''kami'' with Prince Ohiko (大彦命), the ancestor of the Hokuriku ''Kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is mentioned in and entry for 833 AD in ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and per the same source, the shrine was awarded the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) in 842 AD. Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD. The subsequent history of the shrine is uncertain and often contradictory, as most old records have been lost in fires and other disasters over the years. The shrine was well patronised by the military class, and the shrine treasury has a [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] [[:en:Ōdachi|Ōdachi]] [[Japanese sword]] which is an [[:en:Important Cultural Property of Japan|Important Cultural Property of Japan]] as well as amor and swords donated by [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]].<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161113214513/http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html |date=2016-11-13 }}; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Takada Domain|Takada Domain]], [[Matsudaira Tadateru]] granted the shrine estates with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''koku'' for its upkeep/ During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholar [[Hirata Atsutane]] claimed that the shrine had preserved in ''[[:en:Jindai moji|Jindai moji]]'', predating the introduction of Chinese-based ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'', but that these records had been lost in a fire.
After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Yahiko Station|Yahiko Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Yahiko Line|Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:弥彦山.jpg|Mount Yahiko File:Yahiko Jinja2008-2.jpg|Torii Iyahiko-jinja Tohashira-jinja.JPG|Tobashira Jinja Shaden </gallery>
==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社Keidai-Massha十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Ichinomiya]]

It is one of the three shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The shrine's annual festival is held on February 2.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref> This shrine standing at the foot of a mountain is popularly known as a power spot for love and good fortune<ref name="planetyze.com">{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/niigata/yahiko-shrine/information|title=About Yahiko Shrine - Niigata Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-10-14}}</ref> The shrine is located within [[:en:Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park|Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park]] and is on the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]],<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> a 634-meter [[:en:Sacred mountains|sacred mountain]] which forms the ''[[shintai]]'' of the shrine.
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Yahiko Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto'''|天香山命||}}
==History== The foundation date of Yahiko Shrine is unknown, but the shrine dates to prehistoric times as it us referred to as "ancient" in a poem even in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]''. Per the shrine's legend, Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto landed from the heavens at Nozumihama (in what is now the city of [[:en:Nagaoka, Niigata|Nagaoka]]) and taught local people about industries such as fishing, salt production, rice cultivation, and [[:en:Sericulture|Sericulture]].<ref name="planetyze.com"/><ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> He was later enshrined on Mount Yahiko as the ''kami'' who founded Echigo. He also was recorded in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as having performed a ''[[:en:Bugaku|Bugaku]]'' dance at the coronation of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]. Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto is also claimed to be the ancestor of the Owari ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' and it is more than likely that the shrine legend and tradition confuses this ''kami'' with Prince Ohiko (大彦命), the ancestor of the Hokuriku ''Kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is mentioned in and entry for 833 AD in ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and per the same source, the shrine was awarded the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) in 842 AD. Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD. The subsequent history of the shrine is uncertain and often contradictory, as most old records have been lost in fires and other disasters over the years. The shrine was well patronised by the military class, and the shrine treasury has a [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] [[:en:Ōdachi|Ōdachi]] [[Japanese sword]] which is an [[:en:Important Cultural Property of Japan|Important Cultural Property of Japan]] as well as amor and swords donated by [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]].<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161113214513/http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html |date=2016-11-13 }}; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Takada Domain|Takada Domain]], [[Matsudaira Tadateru]] granted the shrine estates with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''koku'' for its upkeep/ During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholar [[Hirata Atsutane]] claimed that the shrine had preserved in ''[[:en:Jindai moji|Jindai moji]]'', predating the introduction of Chinese-based ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'', but that these records had been lost in a fire.
After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Yahiko Station|Yahiko Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Yahiko Line|Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:弥彦山.jpg|Mount Yahiko File:Yahiko Jinja2008-2.jpg|Torii Iyahiko-jinja Tohashira-jinja.JPG|Tobashira Jinja Shaden </gallery>
==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社Keidai-Massha十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Iyahiko-jinja]][[en:Yahiko Shrine]][[fr:Yahiko-jinja]][[ja:彌彦神社]][[ko:이야히코 신사]][[zh:彌彥神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q195684]] Wikidata name: Yahiko Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yahiko Shrine |native_name = 弥彦神社 | image = File:Iyahiko-jinja 1.JPG | caption = Haiden of Yahiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37.7066|N|138.826|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yahiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Kaguyama|ja|天香山命|zh|天香山命}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko, Niigata]], Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://www.yahiko-jinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = February 2 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} | location = {{ill|Nishikanbara district|WD=Q1155139}}; {{ill|Yahiko|WD=Q1349667}} | part_of = {{ill|San Hikoyama|WD=Q11355405}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yahiko jinja'''|弥彦神社||}}, also known as '''Iyahiko-jinja''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Yahiko neighborhood of the village of [[:en:Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], [[:en:Nishikanbara District, Niigata|Nishikanbara District]], [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the three shrines which claim the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2012-3-13.</ref> The shrine's annual festival is held on February 2.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref> This shrine standing at the foot of a mountain is popularly known as a power spot for love and good fortune<ref name="planetyze.com">{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/niigata/yahiko-shrine/information|title=About Yahiko Shrine - Niigata Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-10-14}}</ref> The shrine is located within [[:en:Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park|Sado-Yahiko-Yoneyama Quasi-National Park]] and is on the eastern base of [[Mount Yahiko]],<ref>Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO), [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/regional/niigata/yahikosan.html "Mt. Yahiko Area"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> a 634-meter [[:en:Sacred mountains|sacred mountain]] which forms the ''[[shintai]]'' of the shrine.
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Yahiko Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto'''|天香山命||}}
==History== The foundation date of Yahiko Shrine is unknown, but the shrine dates to prehistoric times as it us referred to as "ancient" in a poem even in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]''. Per the shrine's legend, Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto landed from the heavens at Nozumihama (in what is now the city of [[:en:Nagaoka, Niigata|Nagaoka]]) and taught local people about industries such as fishing, salt production, rice cultivation, and [[:en:Sericulture|Sericulture]].<ref name="planetyze.com"/><ref>JNTO, [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/yahikojinja.html "Yahiko-jinja Shrine"]; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yahiko-jinja/ "Yahiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref> He was later enshrined on Mount Yahiko as the ''kami'' who founded Echigo. He also was recorded in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as having performed a ''[[:en:Bugaku|Bugaku]]'' dance at the coronation of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]. Ame-no-Kaguyama-no-mikoto is also claimed to be the ancestor of the Owari ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' and it is more than likely that the shrine legend and tradition confuses this ''kami'' with Prince Ohiko (大彦命), the ancestor of the Hokuriku ''Kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is mentioned in and entry for 833 AD in ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and per the same source, the shrine was awarded the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) in 842 AD. Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD. The subsequent history of the shrine is uncertain and often contradictory, as most old records have been lost in fires and other disasters over the years. The shrine was well patronised by the military class, and the shrine treasury has a [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] [[:en:Ōdachi|Ōdachi]] [[Japanese sword]] which is an [[:en:Important Cultural Property of Japan|Important Cultural Property of Japan]] as well as amor and swords donated by [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]].<ref name="village">[http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/ Yahiko Village website], [http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html "Sightseeing Information--Yahiko Shrine"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161113214513/http://www.vill.yahiko.niigata.jp/language/main-kankou_E.html |date=2016-11-13 }}; retrieved 2012-10-24.</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Takada Domain|Takada Domain]], [[Matsudaira Tadateru]] granted the shrine estates with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''koku'' for its upkeep/ During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholar [[Hirata Atsutane]] claimed that the shrine had preserved in ''[[:en:Jindai moji|Jindai moji]]'', predating the introduction of Chinese-based ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'', but that these records had been lost in a fire.
After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1871. The present shrine structures were rebuilt in 1916. The earlier shrine buildings were destroyed by a 1912 fire which started in the village.<ref name="village"/>
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Yahiko Station|Yahiko Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Yahiko Line|Yahiko Line]].<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:弥彦山.jpg|Mount Yahiko File:Yahiko Jinja2008-2.jpg|Torii Iyahiko-jinja Tohashira-jinja.JPG|Tobashira Jinja Shaden </gallery>
==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社Keidai-Massha十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Ōdachi'''|大太刀||}}, Muromachi period, dated 1415, blade length of 220.4 cm. <ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6160|title=大太刀〈銘南無正八幡大菩薩右恵門烝家盛／南無唵麾利支天源定重応永廿二年十二月日〉|trans-title= Ōdachi <signed Sho-Hachiman Daibosatsu Uemon-no-jo Iemori / Nasu Marishaten Minamoto no Sadashige Ōe Nijuni-nen, Junigatsu-hi>|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''Tetsu Bussho-bachi'''|鉄仏餉鉢||}}, Kamakura period, dated 1326<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6749|title=鉄仏餉鉢〉|trans-title= Tetsu Bussho-bachi <|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Ichinomiya]]

==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Tobashira Jinja Shaden'''|十柱神社社殿||}}, Edo Period, completed in 1694. This is a sub-shrine located within the precincts of the Yahiko Jinja with a thatched ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' roof. It was designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1917.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/646|title=弥彦神社Keidai-Massha十柱神社社殿|trans-title=Yahiko jinja keidai massha Tobashira Jinja Shaden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
